mnemonics.title: PERSONA Blueprint
mnemonics.content: P-Purpose (why they use product), E-Emotions (feelings), R-Research (data source), S-Goals/Frustrations, O-Organization (demographics), N-Name/Photo (make memorable), A-Actions (behaviors)

mnemonics.title: Primary Gets Priority
mnemonics.content: Primary personas get priority in ALL design decisions. If a design choice conflicts between primary and secondary, prioritize primary.

mnemonics.title: Persona Creation FLOW
mnemonics.content: F-Find users, L-Learn about them, O-Organize data, W-Write personas - keeps the process memorable

acronyms.expansion: Persona Elements: Tasks, Emotions, Actions, Goals - covers what to include in persona behavior analysis
acronyms.usage: When analyzing user research data, organize insights using PETA framework

acronyms.expansion: Know your Persona Inside-out - reminder to truly understand personas before designing
acronyms.usage: Before design meetings, review persona details to keep user needs top of mind

acronyms.expansion: Research Driven Marketing (or Design) - emphasizes research-based persona creation
acronyms.usage: Avoid assumption-based personas; use RDM approach

visualTips.visualization: Think of a pyramid: Primary at top (most important), Secondary in middle, Negative at bottom (excluded). This hierarchy guides decision priority.
A funnel shape: Wide at top (lots of user data from research), narrowing down to 3-5 distinct persona groups at the bottom.
Left brain (profile) = data/numbers; Right brain (persona) = stories/emotions. Both needed but serve different purposes.
Empathy Map → Persona → Scenario. Like a pipeline: raw insights (empathy map) → refined character (persona) → story (scenario).

analogies.analogy: Like a character in a movie script - has background, motivations, fears, and goals. Directors use character sheets to ensure consistent, relatable performances.
Like a lead actor vs supporting cast. The lead gets the most screen time and story focus, but supporting characters add depth.
Like a map based on actual exploration vs a map drawn from imagination. One leads you to real treasure; the other may lead nowhere.
Like cooking for a specific guest - you ask about preferences, allergies, and tastes before planning the menu. Personas are those preferences.
Like giving a character a name and backstory in a novel - makes them memorable and real, not just another face in a crowd.
